blocks.text: Why do you boast, O mighty one, of mischief done against the godly? All day long

Ps 52: True confidence. A contrast between a self‐confident wicked warrior and a person who trusts God. The psalmist denounces the arrogant (vv. 1–4), confidently awaits God’s punishment of them (vv. 5–7), and concludes with a declaration of trust in God (vv. 8–9). It is similar to Ps 36.
1 : , sometimes applied to God, here it is ironically applied to the self‐important deceiver whose days are numbered.
you are plotting destruction. Your tongue is like a sharp razor, you worker of treachery.
You love evil more than good, and lying more than speaking the truth. Selah
You love all words that devour, O deceitful tongue.
1–4 : The psalm uses types, typical ways of behaving (e.g., the wicked plot against the good person), a style common in wisdom literature.
But God will break you down forever; he will snatch and tear you from your tent; he will uproot you from the land of the living. Selah
The righteous will see, and fear, and will laugh at the evildoer, saying,
6 : By effecting justice in the world, God encourages people to act justly. There is a pun on similar‐sounding Heb roots, “yrʾ”(“to fear”) and “rʾh” (“to see”).
“See the one who would not take refuge in God, but trusted in abundant riches, and sought refuge in wealth!”
5–7 : Justice must not only be done, it must be seen. The sinner will be excluded from the Temple, but the psalmist will be “like a green olive tree in the house of God” (v. 8).
But I am like a green olive tree in the house of God. I trust in the steadfast love of God forever and ever.
8 : , abruptly shifts attention to the psalmist who enjoys the protection of the Temple. Ancient temples often had gardens that symbolized the life‐giving capacity of the god worshiped there.
I will thank you forever, because of what you have done. In the presence of the faithful I will proclaim your name, for it is good.
